messages.content: hi
Hello! How can I assist you today?
please correct my article
Sure, I would be happy to help you. Please provide me with the article and let me know what kind of corrections do you need.

Abstract— Due to frequent faults occurring in an inverter, it will be important to detect faults early and find a way to control operation and preventive action to avoid recurrent interruptions. This study develops a technique based on pattern recognition in single and multiple faults switch in three-phase inverter fed induction motor using artificial intelligence techniques. In particular, the work aims to examine the possibility of both a single and multiple faults switch detection, location, and diagnosis in a three-phase inverter feed an induction motor drive, using the neural network technique. We propose a method based on a pattern recognition technique for open circuit switching faults of voltage inverter, with a simple feature extractor to make detection and diagnosis faults possible and straightforward using a neural network. Simulation results show that developing basic direct torque control of induction motor drive via neural network also detects and localizes single and multiple faults using neural networks. Classification performances for single and multiple faults improved by using a neural network for fault detection and pattern recognition.

Sure, here is the corrected article:

Abstract - Due to frequent faults occurring in inverters, early detection and finding ways to control operation and take preventive actions are important to avoid recurrent interruptions. This study develops a technique based on pattern recognition in the single and multiple faults switch in a three-phase inverter fed induction motor using artificial intelligence techniques. In particular, the work aims to examine the possibility of detecting, locating, and diagnosing both single and multiple faults switches in a three-phase inverter fed an induction motor drive using the neural network technique. We propose a method based on a pattern recognition technique for open circuit switching faults of voltage inverters with simple feature extractors to enable easy detection and diagnosis of faults using a neural network. Simulation results show that developing basic direct torque control of an induction motor drive via a neural network also detects and localizes single and multiple faults using neural networks. The classification performances for single and multiple faults improved by using a neural network for fault detection and pattern recognition.

Direct torque controller drives (DTC) were introduced more than 35 years ago in AC drives. This control strategy was confirmed by (Takahashi’s [1] and Depenbrock’s [2]) [3].
Many artificial intelligence (AI) techniques, such as fuzzy logic control (FLC) [4-8], adaptive neuro-fuzzy interference system (ANFIS) [9-11], artificial neural networks (ANN) [12-15] have been developed and used to control induction motors.

Here's the corrected article with minor changes:

Direct torque controller drives (DTC) were introduced more than 35 years ago in AC drives. This control strategy was confirmed by Takahashi [1] and Depenbrock [2]. Over the years, many artificial intelligence (AI) techniques, such as fuzzy logic control (FLC) [4-8], adaptive neuro-fuzzy interference system (ANFIS) [9-11], and artificial neural networks (ANN) [12-15], have been developed and used to control induction motors.

Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are inspired from the functioning of the human brain can be used for machine learning and artificial intelligence. With these networks (ANN) is to some extent modelled on the structure of the biological brain. It consists of an abstracted model of interconnected neurons, whose special arrangement and linking can be used to solve computer-based application problems in various fields. The neural network is a research subject of Neuron informatics and part of the artificial intelligence and must be trained before they can solve problems [16].

Here's the corrected article:

Artificial Neural Networks (ANN) are inspired by the functioning of the human brain and can be used for machine learning and artificial intelligence. ANN models are to some extent modeled on the structure of the biological brain. They consist of an abstracted model of interconnected neurons, whose special arrangement and linking can be used to solve computer-based application problems in various fields. Neural networks are a research subject of neuronal informatics and are part of artificial intelligence. Before they can solve problems, they must be trained [16].

I hope that helps! Let me know if you need further assistance.

Many faults occur in different elements in AC drive, like fault in the machine (stator fault, rotor); Fault in the drive chain (mating defect, sensors); Power supply fault (imbalance of power supply, fault in the power electronics converter) [17], a fault tolerance control [15]. In this study, we consider the switching devices because of their feebleness and likely to be damaged by faults) [18-21]. Various faults diagnosis schemes used in electrical machine drives are developed and published [22-25].
This work aims to study the possibility of fault-finding via artificial neural networks and feature extraction method. We will make a feature extraction technique to define both single and multiple fault switch signatures. Defect classification based on neural networks will be set to find and localize multiple faults in the PWM inverter.

Here's the corrected article:

Many faults can occur in different elements of an AC drive, such as faults in the machine (stator fault, rotor fault), faults in the drive chain (mating defects, sensors), power supply faults (imbalance of power supply, faults in the power electronics converter) [17], and fault tolerance control [15]. In this study, we focus on the switching devices since they are relatively weak and more likely to be damaged by faults [18-21]. Various fault diagnosis schemes used in electrical machine drives have been developed and published [22-25].

This work aims to explore the possibility of fault detection using artificial neural networks and a feature extraction method. We will develop a feature extraction technique to define both single and multiple fault switch signatures. The defect classification based on neural networks will be used to identify and localize multiple faults in the PWM inverter.

I hope that helps! Let me know if you need further assistance.

This paper is structured as follows: Section 2 developing basic DTC into neural DTC Section 3 introduces the problem statement and analyses the fault diagnosis system under single and multiple open switches. Section 4 presents the proposed method for fault diagnosis. Section 5 presents simulation results. Section 6 presents the conclusion.

Here's the corrected article:

This paper is structured as follows: Section 2 describes the development of basic DTC into neural DTC. Section 3 introduces the problem statement and analyzes the fault diagnosis system under single and multiple open switches. Section 4 presents the proposed method for fault diagnosis. Section 5 presents the simulation results. Finally, Section 6 concludes the paper.

As seen in Figure 1, two loops correspond to the stator flux and torque magnitudes. The reference values (Te*, Φs*) for the torque and the flux compared with the actual values (Φs, Te) induce errors (εT, εᵩ). These errors feed two hysteresis blocks respectively (see Figure 2). Outputs from the two hysteresis blocks with the stator flux position are used as inputs of the switching table. The inverter is switched on using these errors and the position of the stator flux over six region control; inverter in six-step operation. It has six active vectors and two zero vectors, as shown in Figure 3. So that the inverter output voltage vector minimizes the flux and the torque errors and defines the direction of the flux rotation.

Here's the corrected article:

As shown in Figure 1, there are two control loops corresponding to the stator flux and torque magnitudes. The reference values (Te*, Φs*) for the torque and flux are compared with the actual values (Φs, Te) to induce errors (εT, εᵩ). These errors are inputs to two hysteresis blocks respectively (see Figure 2). The outputs from the two hysteresis blocks and the stator flux position are used as inputs to the switching table. The inverter is switched on using these errors and the position of the stator flux via six-region control. This results in the inverter operating in six-step operation with six active vectors and two zero vectors, as shown in Figure 3. The inverter output voltage vector is adjusted to minimize the flux and torque errors and define the direction of the flux rotation.

The position of the stator flux vector, which determines the corresponding sector S, is crucial in selecting the correct voltage vector (refer to Table 1). The chosen voltage vector can either decrease or increase the stator magnetic flux or torque estimation [3].

The choice of the vector Us is determined by the flux module's of the desired variation, as well as the desired changes in rotation speed and torque. If the voltage vector is chosen correctly, the stator flux can be controlled. The stator flux plane is divided into six sectors, each with a set of voltage vectors [31] as shown in Figure 3.

Here's the corrected article:

The choice of the vector Us is determined by the desired variation in the flux magnitude, as well as the desired changes in the rotation speed and torque. If the voltage vector is chosen correctly, the stator flux can be controlled. The stator flux plane is divided into six sectors, each with a corresponding set of voltage vectors [31], as shown in Figure 3.

When the flux is in the zone i, the vector Vi+1 or Vi-1 is selected to increase the level of the flux, and Vi+2 or Vi-2 is selected to decrease it. At the same time, the vector Vi+1 or Vi+2 is selected to increase the level of the torque, and Vi-1 or Vi-2 is selected to decrease it. If V0 or V7 is selected, the rotation of the flux is stopped and the torque decreases. Whereas the amplitude of flux remains unchanged (see Table 2).

Here's the corrected article:

When the flux is in zone i, the vector Vi+1 or Vi-1 is selected to increase the magnitude of the flux, and Vi+2 or Vi-2 is selected to decrease it. At the same time, the vector Vi+1 or Vi+2 is selected to increase the torque, and Vi-1 or Vi-2 is selected to decrease it. If V0 or V7 is selected, the flux rotation is stopped, and the torque decreases. The amplitude of the flux, however, remains unchanged (see Table 2).

A multilayer perceptron (MLP) is used to replace the two hysteresis blocs and the switch table due to the continuous features of the input data. The sigmoid activation function is used for hidden nodes and an output node [26].

Here's the corrected article:

A multilayer perceptron (MLP) with a sigmoid activation function for both hidden nodes and the output node is used to replace the two hysteresis blocks and the switch table. This is due to the continuous features of the input data [26].

The network has three layers, an input layer contain three neurons corresponding three input (εT, εΦ, Θs), tow hidden layer, and an output layer contain three neurons corresponding three output (Sa,Sb,Sc). After many simulations, we have found that 7 neurons in the first hidden layer and 3 neurons in the second hidden layer is the best configuration. As shown in Figure 5.

Here's the corrected article:

The network used in this study has three layers: an input layer containing three neurons corresponding to the three inputs (εT, εΦ, and Θs), two hidden layers, and an output layer containing three neurons corresponding to the three outputs (Sa, Sb, and Sc). After numerous simulations, it was found that 7 neurons in the first hidden layer and 3 neurons in the second hidden layer provide the best configuration. This is exemplified in Figure 5.

The Levenberg Marquardt training paradigm is used in this research because of its rapid training time and regular characteristic properties.
The 5% misclassification and 5% input data error rate are chosen to calculate a sum of square error goal, SSE. The training process is finished when the 0.01 SSE goal is met [9].

Here's the corrected article:

The Levenberg-Marquardt training algorithm is used in this research due to its fast training time and regular characteristic properties. A sum of squared error goal (SSE) is calculated using a 5% misclassification rate and a 5% input data error rate. The training process is considered complete when the SSE goal of 0.01 is achieved [9].

I hope that helps! Let me know if you need further assistance.
Where, yi is the output value of the target and di is the output of the training data, N is the training data number.

Here's the corrected article:

Here, yi represents the target output value, di is the output of the training data, and N is the number of training data samples.

Training Data: For each input pattern (εT, εΦ, Θs), we collect three rows of data with 1500000 columns each. Simulated test data sets with varying speed references are used to create the test data sets. In this project, 25% of the input/output data is used for validation and 25% is used for testing. The number of off-line training epochs required to achieve a 0.0428 error is 465 epochs, as shown in Figure 6.

Here's the corrected article:

Training Data: For each input pattern (εT, εΦ, Θs), three rows of data are collected with 1,500,000 columns each. To create the test data sets, simulated test data sets with varying speed references are used. In this project, 25% of the input/output data is used for validation and 25% is used for testing. The number of offline training epochs required to achieve a 0.0428 error is 465 epochs, as shown in Figure 6.

To see the effect of an open switch fault, consider the case when the switch T1 is open. The current cannot flow in the phase A through the upper bridge arm when this current is positive; the output current of phase A is zero. During this half period, the phase current of the remaining phases are distorted due to the three-phase current balance. When the phase current is in the negative range, the current can flow in the phase A through the lower bridge arm via the freewheel diode D4. The three currents have no distortion in this half-period. The same reasoning remains valid for the other switches [31].

Here's the corrected article:

To observe the effect of an open switch fault, consider the case when switch T1 is open. During a half period in which the current through phase A is positive, the current cannot flow through the upper bridge arm, leading to a zero output current for phase A. As a result of the three-phase current balance, the phase current for the remaining phases is distorted during this half-period. However, when the phase current is in the negative range, current can flow through phase A via the lower bridge arm through the freewheel diode D4. During this half-period, the three-phase currents experience no distortion. The same applies to the other switches [31].

I hope that helps! Let me know if you need further assistance.

In the case of a double switch fault in the same bridge arm, the current in the considered phase is zero. Consequently, the currents of the other phases are distorted; they are out of phase. For example if T1 and T4 are open, Isb(t)=-Isc(t) [31].

Here's the corrected article:
